title: Muscle Energetics
expanded_description: An introduction to how muscles get the energy they need to contract. This guide explains the three energy systems muscles use: the immediate system (creatine phosphate), the short-term system (glycolysis), and the long-term system (oxidative phosphorylation). It covers how these systems work, when they are used, and how they affect performance and fatigue.

core_concept.principle: Muscles need a constant supply of ATP (energy) to contract. They have three energy systems that work together to produce ATP: the creatine phosphate system (immediate energy, lasts 5-10 seconds), glycolysis (short-term energy, lasts 30-90 seconds, produces lactate), and oxidative phosphorylation (long-term energy, lasts hours, uses oxygen). The system used depends on how hard and how long you exercise.
core_concept.explanation:
Your muscles are like engines that need fuel to run. The fuel for muscles is a molecule called ATP (adenosine triphosphate). When a muscle contracts, it breaks down ATP into ADP and phosphate, releasing energy. But your muscles only store enough ATP for 1-2 seconds of maximum effort! So they need to constantly make new ATP.

Muscles have three ways to make ATP, like three different engines:

1. Creatine Phosphate System (The Sprinter's Engine):
• Uses creatine phosphate (CP) stored in muscles.
• Very fast—makes ATP in milliseconds.
• Limited fuel—lasts only 5-10 seconds.
• No waste products.
• Used for: 100-meter sprint, jumping, heavy weightlifting.

2. Glycolysis (The Middle-Distance Engine):
• Breaks down sugar (glycogen) without oxygen.
• Fast—makes ATP in seconds.
• Lasts 30-90 seconds.
• Produces lactate (lactic acid) as a byproduct.
• Used for: 400-meter run, 100-meter swim, repeated sprints.

3. Oxidative Phosphorylation (The Marathon Engine):
• Uses oxygen to break down sugar or fat.
• Slower—takes time to ramp up.
• Can last for hours.
• Produces CO₂ and water (no lactate).
• Used for: long-distance running, cycling, hiking.

These systems don't work alone—they all work together. When you start exercising, all three turn on. But which one provides most of the ATP depends on how hard you're going and how long you've been going.

Fiber types also matter:
• Type I (slow-twitch) fibers are built for endurance—they have lots of mitochondria (the cell's power plants) and use oxygen.
• Type IIb (fast-twitch) fibers are built for power—they store lots of glycogen and use glycolysis.
• Type IIa fibers are in between—they can do both.

Fun fact: Your muscles store about 1,200-1,600 calories of glycogen (sugar). That's enough for about 90 minutes of running. When it runs out, marathon runners "hit the wall"—they suddenly feel exhausted because their muscles are out of fuel.

image.alt: Three energy systems: Creatine phosphate (immediate, 5-10 sec), Glycolysis (short-term, 30-90 sec, produces lactate), Oxidative phosphorylation (long-term, hours, uses oxygen). Graph showing power vs. duration.
image.caption: Three energy systems: Creatine phosphate (sprint), Glycolysis (400m), Oxidative phosphorylation (marathon). Each has different power and duration.

sections.introduction:
Every movement you make—from blinking to sprinting—requires energy. That energy comes from a molecule called ATP (adenosine triphosphate). Think of ATP as the "gasoline" that powers your muscles. When a muscle contracts, it breaks ATP into ADP and phosphate, releasing energy.

The problem: Your muscles only store about 5 grams of ATP—enough for 1-2 seconds of maximum effort! So your muscles must constantly make new ATP. They have three different "engines" to do this, each suited for different activities.

Why three engines? Because different activities have different energy demands. A 100-meter sprint needs a huge burst of energy very quickly. A marathon needs a steady supply of energy for hours. Your muscles have evolved three systems to handle these different demands.

The three energy systems:
• Creatine phosphate system (the "sprinter's engine"): very fast, lasts 5-10 seconds.
• Glycolysis (the "middle-distance engine"): fast, lasts 30-90 seconds, produces lactate.
• Oxidative phosphorylation (the "marathon engine"): slower, can last for hours, uses oxygen.

These systems work together. When you start exercising, all three turn on. But depending on how hard you're going, one system will be the main supplier of ATP.

Muscle fibers are also specialized for different energy systems:
• Type I (slow-twitch): have many mitochondria, use oxygen, great for endurance.
• Type IIb (fast-twitch): store lots of sugar (glycogen), use glycolysis, great for power.
• Type IIa (mixed): can do both.

Understanding how your muscles make energy helps athletes train smarter, helps people understand fatigue, and helps explain why some people are better at sprinting and others at distance running.

introduction_image.alt: Diagram showing ATP being used by myosin head (contraction) and calcium pump. ATP → ADP + Pi releases energy.
introduction_image.caption: Muscles use ATP for energy. ATP is broken down into ADP and phosphate, releasing energy for contraction. Muscles must constantly make new ATP.

key_concept.definition: Muscles have three energy systems: creatine phosphate (immediate, 5-10 sec), glycolysis (short-term, 30-90 sec, produces lactate), and oxidative phosphorylation (long-term, sustained, uses oxygen). The system used depends on exercise intensity and duration.
key_concept.context:
Think of your muscles as having three gears. First gear (creatine phosphate) is for explosive starts. Second gear (glycolysis) is for sustained power. Third gear (oxidative) is for endurance cruising. You use all gears, but which one is dominant changes with how long you've been going.

Key facts:
• 100-meter sprint (10 sec): 50-70% from creatine phosphate, 30-40% from glycolysis.
• 400-meter run (45-60 sec): 50-60% from glycolysis.
• Marathon (>2 hours): over 95% from oxidative phosphorylation.

Real-world connection: Sprinters train to increase their creatine phosphate stores and glycolytic enzymes. Marathon runners train to increase their mitochondria and fat-burning ability. The training matches the energy system they need.

image.alt: Graph showing energy system contribution over time: 0-10 sec (CP dominant), 10-90 sec (glycolysis dominant), >2 min (oxidative dominant).
image.caption: Energy system contribution changes with exercise duration. CP system dominates sprints; glycolysis dominates 400m runs; oxidative dominates marathons.

sections.key_concept: The three energy systems—creatine phosphate (immediate), glycolysis (short-term anaerobic), and oxidative phosphorylation (long-term aerobic)—work together to meet ATP demands, with the dominant system determined by exercise intensity and duration.

classifications.type: Creatine Phosphate System: The Sprinter's Engine
characteristics: Reaction: Creatine phosphate (CP) + ADP → creatine + ATP
ATP yield: 1 ATP per CP molecule.
Waste: None (just creatine, which is recycled).
classifications.behavior:
The creatine phosphate system is your muscle's emergency power. It's like a battery that can deliver a huge burst of energy instantly—but it runs out quickly.

How it works: Your muscles store a molecule called creatine phosphate (CP). When you need quick energy, an enzyme called creatine kinase (CK) grabs a phosphate from CP and attaches it to ADP, making ATP. This happens in milliseconds.

How much do you have? Your muscles store about 4-6 times more CP than ATP. That's enough for 5-10 seconds of all-out effort—a 100-meter sprint, a heavy lift, or a single jump.

What happens when you use it? CP levels drop quickly. After 10 seconds of sprinting, CP stores are 70-80% depleted. The creatine that's left behind is harmless and will be recycled later.

Recovery: After you stop, your muscles use oxygen to rebuild CP. It takes about 20-30 seconds to restore half your CP, and 3-5 minutes for full recovery. This is why sprinters need rest between repetitions—they need to recharge their CP battery.

Clinical connection: Doctors measure creatine kinase (CK) in blood as a marker of muscle damage. When muscles are injured (like in a crush injury or muscular dystrophy), CK leaks out, and blood levels go way up.

examples.explanation:
Creatine is a popular supplement among athletes. Here's how it works:
(1) Normal: Your muscles store CP naturally. The amount you have is limited by how much creatine you get from food (meat, fish) and what your body makes.
(2) Supplementation: Taking creatine (5-20 grams per day for a week) can increase muscle CP stores by 10-20%.
(3) Effect: More CP means more immediate energy. This helps in sports that require repeated bursts of effort—sprinting, weightlifting, soccer, football.
(4) Who benefits: Athletes in sports with repeated high-intensity efforts (interval training, team sports).
(5) Safety: Creatine is generally safe for healthy adults, but it's not for everyone. Always consult a doctor before starting supplements.

Why this matters: Creatine is one of the few supplements that has real science behind it. But it only helps for short, explosive activities—it won't make you a better marathon runner.

image.alt: Diagram: Creatine supplement → increased CP stores → more ATP during sprint. Graph showing performance improvement in repeated sprints.
image.caption: Creatine supplements increase muscle CP stores by 10-20%. This helps in sports with repeated bursts of effort (sprinting, weightlifting, team sports).

classifications.value: Understanding energy for 400-meter runs, swimming, and repeated efforts (30-120 seconds)
characteristics: Process: Breaks down sugar (glycogen/glucose) without oxygen.
ATP yield: 2-3 ATP per sugar molecule.
Waste: Produces lactate (lactic acid) and H⁺ (acid).
classifications.behavior:
When your CP runs out (after about 10 seconds), your muscles turn to glycolysis—breaking down sugar without oxygen. This is your second gear.

How it works: Your muscles store sugar in a form called glycogen. Glycolysis breaks glycogen into pyruvate, producing a small amount of ATP (2-3 per sugar). When oxygen is low, pyruvate is converted to lactate (lactic acid).

Why lactate matters: Lactate is often blamed for muscle soreness, but that's not quite right. Lactate itself is actually fuel for other muscles and your heart. The problem is the H⁺ (acid) that comes with it. As acid builds up, your muscle pH drops from 7.0 to about 6.4-6.6. This acid interferes with the enzymes that make ATP, causing fatigue. The burning sensation you feel at the end of a hard run is from this acid buildup.

How much can you make? Your muscles store about 300-400 grams of glycogen—enough for about 90 minutes of moderate exercise. During hard effort, you burn through it much faster.

Lactate threshold: As you exercise harder, lactate starts to accumulate in your blood. The point where it starts to rise rapidly is your lactate threshold. Trained athletes have a higher lactate threshold—they can go faster before lactate builds up. This is why they can run faster without fatiguing.

Clinical connection: McArdle disease is a genetic disorder where people can't break down muscle glycogen. They have severe exercise intolerance—their muscles cramp and break down during high-intensity exercise. This shows how important glycolysis is for power activities.

examples.explanation:
Lactate threshold is one of the best predictors of endurance performance. Here's why:
(1) What it is: The exercise intensity at which lactate starts to accumulate in the blood.
(2) Untrained person: Lactate threshold at 50-60% of maximum capacity.
(3) Trained athlete: Lactate threshold at 70-85% of maximum capacity.
(4) Why it matters: If you run at 65% of your max, an untrained person will be above threshold (lactate building, fatigue soon). A trained athlete will be below threshold (steady state, can go for hours).
(5) How to improve: Interval training (short, hard efforts with recovery) raises lactate threshold. Long, steady runs also help.

Testing: Sports scientists measure lactate threshold by taking small blood samples during incremental exercise. The intensity at lactate threshold is used to set training paces.

image.alt: Graph: Lactate vs. exercise intensity. Untrained: threshold at 50-60% VO₂max. Trained: threshold at 70-85% VO₂max.
image.caption: Lactate threshold: The intensity where lactate starts to build up. Trained athletes have a higher threshold, allowing them to go faster before fatigue sets in.

classifications.value: Understanding energy for long-duration activities (minutes to hours)
characteristics: Process: Uses oxygen to break down sugar or fat.
Speed: Slower (takes time to ramp up).
ATP yield: 34-36 ATP per sugar, over 100 ATP per fat molecule.
classifications.behavior:
The oxidative system is your endurance engine. It's slower to start than the other systems, but it can run for hours. This is the system that powers you through a long run, bike ride, or hike.

How it works: The oxidative system happens in your mitochondria—the "power plants" of your cells. Sugar (pyruvate) or fat (fatty acids) enter the mitochondria and are broken down using oxygen. This produces a lot of ATP (34-36 per sugar, over 100 per fat) and leaves behind only CO₂ and water.

Substrate choice: Your muscles can burn both sugar and fat. At rest and during easy exercise, you burn mostly fat. As you go harder, you burn more sugar. Why? Because burning sugar produces ATP faster than burning fat. When you need quick energy (like picking up the pace), your muscles switch to sugar.

Glycogen depletion: Your muscles store about 300-400 grams of glycogen (sugar). That's enough for about 90 minutes of hard running. When you run out, you "hit the wall"—sudden, overwhelming fatigue. Marathon runners carbo-load (eat lots of carbohydrates) before a race to pack in extra glycogen.

Endurance training adaptations: Training changes your muscles at the cellular level:
• More mitochondria (50-100% increase).
• More capillaries (more oxygen delivery).
• Better ability to burn fat (spares glycogen).
• Higher lactate threshold.

Clinical connection: Mitochondrial myopathies are genetic disorders that affect the mitochondria. People with these conditions have exercise intolerance and fatigue because their muscles can't use oxygen effectively. They often have high lactate levels even at rest.

examples.process: Endurance Training: Building More Power Plants
examples.explanation:
When you train for endurance, your muscles change at the cellular level:
(1) More mitochondria: Endurance training increases the number of mitochondria in your muscles by 50-100%. More power plants mean more ATP from oxygen.
(2) More capillaries: Your muscles grow more blood vessels to deliver oxygen and remove waste.
(3) Better fat burning: Your muscles become better at using fat for fuel, sparing glycogen for when you need it (like the final push).
(4) Higher lactate threshold: You can go faster before lactate builds up.

How long does it take? You start seeing changes within weeks, but significant adaptations take months. This is why endurance athletes train year-round.

Molecular switch: A protein called PGC-1α is the "master switch" for endurance adaptations. When you exercise, PGC-1α turns on genes that build more mitochondria and more capillaries. This is why exercise changes your muscles at the deepest level.

image.alt: Diagram: Untrained muscle (few mitochondria) → trained muscle (more mitochondria, more capillaries). PGC-1α activation.
image.caption: Endurance training increases mitochondria (power plants) and capillaries (oxygen delivery). A protein called PGC-1α is the master switch for these adaptations.

principles.principle: Power vs. Capacity: You Can't Have Both
principles.statement: The three energy systems have a trade-off: the fastest system (creatine phosphate) has the smallest capacity; the slowest system (oxidative) has the largest capacity. You can't have both high power and long duration from one system.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• You can sprint all-out for only 10 seconds.
• You can run at a moderate pace for hours.
• Training for power (sprinting) and endurance (marathon) are different.

Everyday example: Think of a car. A drag racer has huge power but runs out of fuel in seconds. A hybrid car has less power but can go for hours. Your muscles are the same—different systems for different jobs.
principles.example:
Compare the three systems:
• Creatine phosphate: 10-12 ATP/kg/sec (high power), 50-100 ATP/kg total (low capacity).
• Glycolysis: 5-7 ATP/kg/sec (medium power), 200-300 ATP/kg total (medium capacity).
• Oxidative: 1-2 ATP/kg/sec (low power), unlimited capacity (as long as fuel and oxygen last).

This is why a 100-meter sprinter uses CP; a 400-meter runner uses glycolysis; a marathoner uses oxidative.
principles.analogy: Power vs. capacity is like a camera flash vs. a lantern. The flash is incredibly bright but lasts a split second. The lantern is dimmer but can shine for hours. Your muscles have both—you use the flash when you need it, the lantern when you need endurance.

image.alt: Graph: Power vs. capacity. CP: highest power, lowest capacity. Glycolysis: medium. Oxidative: lowest power, highest capacity.
image.caption: Power-capacity trade-off: The fastest system (CP) has the smallest capacity. The slowest system (oxidative) has the largest capacity. You can't have both.

principles.statement: Muscle fibers are specialized for different energy systems. Type I fibers have many mitochondria and are built for oxidative (endurance) metabolism. Type IIb fibers have lots of glycogen and glycolytic enzymes and are built for power. Type IIa fibers are in between.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Postural muscles (like your calf for standing) have mostly Type I fibers.
• Sprinters have more Type II fibers in their legs.
• Marathon runners have more Type I fibers.
• You can train fibers within a type, but you can't turn Type I into Type II.

Everyday example: A diesel truck engine is built for endurance (Type I). A race car engine is built for power (Type IIb). They are both engines, but they are built differently for different jobs.
principles.example:
Muscle fiber distribution:
• Soleus (postural calf): 70-85% Type I, 15-30% Type II.
• Gastrocnemius (calf used for sprinting): 40-60% Type I, 40-60% Type II.
• Extraocular (eye muscles): 10-20% Type I, 80-90% Type II.

Why does this matter? Your genetics play a big role in your fiber type makeup. Some people are born with more Type I fibers (better at endurance), some with more Type II (better at power). But training can improve both—just not change your basic type.
principles.analogy: Fiber types are like car engines. You can tune a diesel engine to be more powerful, but it will never be a Formula 1 engine. You can tune a race car engine to be more reliable, but it will never match a diesel's fuel economy. Your muscles are the same—you can improve, but your basic type is largely genetic.

image.alt: Type I fiber: many mitochondria, good for endurance. Type IIb fiber: lots of glycogen, good for power.
image.caption: Fiber type specialization: Type I (slow-twitch) has many mitochondria—good for endurance. Type IIb (fast-twitch) stores lots of glycogen—good for power. Your muscles are a mix.

principles.principle: Oxygen Debt: Paying Back After Exercise
principles.statement: After exercise, you continue breathing hard even though you've stopped. This extra oxygen (EPOC, or oxygen debt) is used to replenish CP stores, clear lactate, and restore oxygen levels in your blood and muscles.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• You breathe hard after a sprint, not just during.
• Active recovery (light walking) helps clear lactate faster than sitting.
• Your metabolism stays elevated after exercise, burning extra calories.

Everyday example: After you sprint up stairs, you're breathing hard for minutes afterward. That's your body paying back the oxygen you borrowed during the sprint. It's like paying off a credit card—you used energy you didn't have (anaerobic), and now you're paying it back with interest.
principles.example:
Components of oxygen debt:
• Fast component (30-60 seconds): Replenishes CP stores, restores oxygen to myoglobin and hemoglobin.
• Slow component (minutes to hours): Clears lactate (converted to glucose or oxidized), replenishes glycogen, cools the body.

Active recovery (light walking or cycling) can clear lactate 30-50% faster than sitting still. This is why athletes cool down after a race—it speeds recovery.
principles.analogy: Oxygen debt is like a credit card. During intense exercise, you "borrow" energy (use anaerobic systems). After exercise, you "pay back" with extra oxygen (EPOC). The harder you work, the bigger the debt.

image.alt: Graph: Oxygen consumption during and after exercise. EPOC is the extra oxygen consumed after exercise to pay back the debt.
image.caption: Oxygen debt (EPOC): After exercise, you continue breathing hard to replenish CP stores, clear lactate, and restore oxygen levels. This is why you breathe hard after a sprint.

components.definition: Muscle energetics integrates fuel sources (CP, glycogen, fat), energy systems (CP system, glycolysis, oxidative phosphorylation), and cellular structures (mitochondria, glycogen granules).
components.equation: ATP demand = Myosin ATPase + Calcium pump + Sodium/potassium pump
components.interpretation: Most ATP (70-80%) is used by myosin heads to pull actin filaments. The rest is used to pump calcium back into storage (for relaxation) and to maintain the electrical charge across the muscle membrane.

image.alt: Diagram: CP system (cytoplasm), Glycolysis (cytoplasm), Oxidative phosphorylation (mitochondria). Fuel sources: CP, glycogen, fat.
image.caption: Key components: Creatine phosphate system (immediate), Glycolysis (short-term), Oxidative phosphorylation (long-term). Fuel sources: CP, glycogen, fat.

categories.description: McArdle disease (glycogen storage disease type V) is a genetic disorder where muscles can't break down glycogen. People with this condition have severe exercise intolerance—their muscles cramp and can even break down during high-intensity exercise.
categories.detailed_mechanism: In McArdle disease, the enzyme myophosphorylase is missing. This enzyme is needed to break glycogen into glucose for glycolysis. Without it, muscles can't use their stored sugar during intense exercise. They rely entirely on blood glucose and fat, which are much slower energy sources.

examples.explanation:
People with McArdle disease have a strange experience called the "second wind."
(1) Start exercising: They quickly develop muscle pain, cramps, and fatigue because they can't use glycogen.
(2) After 5-10 minutes: Suddenly, they feel better. Their muscles loosen up, and they can continue.
(3) Why? It takes time for the heart to increase blood flow and deliver more oxygen. Once oxygen delivery improves, they can use fat and blood glucose for energy. Their muscles shift from anaerobic to aerobic metabolism.

This is the opposite of normal—most people feel worse as exercise continues, but McArdle patients feel better after a few minutes.

Diagnosis: The ischemic forearm test (squeezing a grip while a blood pressure cuff is inflated) shows no rise in blood lactate in McArdle patients, confirming they can't break down glycogen.

image.alt: Graph: Exercise capacity over time. McArdle patients start poorly, then improve (second wind) as cardiovascular system catches up.
image.caption: McArdle disease: Patients can't break down muscle glycogen. They experience a "second wind" after 5-10 minutes as their cardiovascular system delivers oxygen to use fat and blood glucose.

categories.type: Mitochondrial Myopathies: When the Power Plants Fail
categories.description: Mitochondrial myopathies are genetic disorders affecting the mitochondria (the cell's power plants). People with these conditions have exercise intolerance, fatigue, and often high lactate levels because their muscles can't use oxygen effectively.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Mitochondria contain the machinery for oxidative phosphorylation. Mutations in mitochondrial DNA or nuclear DNA can impair this machinery. Without functioning mitochondria, muscles can't produce enough ATP from oxygen, so they rely more on glycolysis, producing excess lactate.

examples.explanation:
A key finding in mitochondrial myopathies is "ragged red fibers" on muscle biopsy.
(1) What they are: When stained with a special dye (modified Gomori trichrome), muscle fibers with defective mitochondria show red patches—these are clumps of abnormal mitochondria.
(2) Why they happen: The muscle tries to compensate for defective mitochondria by making more of them. But these new mitochondria don't work properly.
(3) Who gets it: People with MELAS, Kearns-Sayre syndrome, and other mitochondrial disorders.

MELAS is a specific mitochondrial disorder with symptoms including:
• Exercise intolerance and fatigue.
• Lactic acidosis (high lactate in blood).
• Stroke-like episodes (not caused by blocked arteries).
• Seizures, hearing loss, diabetes.

Treatment: No cure, but supplements like CoQ10, riboflavin, and L-carnitine may help. Exercise training can also improve function by stimulating new mitochondria.

image.alt: Muscle biopsy with ragged red fibers (red patches). Diagram of defective mitochondria trying to compensate.
image.caption: Mitochondrial myopathies: Defective mitochondria cause muscle fatigue and high lactate. Muscle biopsy shows "ragged red fibers"—clumps of abnormal mitochondria.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Energy Systems in Different Sports

worked_examples.title: Case Study: Energy Systems in Different Events
worked_examples.problem:
Three athletes ask for training advice:
- Athlete A: 100-meter sprinter (10 seconds).
- Athlete B: 800-meter runner (2 minutes).
- Athlete C: Marathon runner (4 hours).
(1) Which energy system dominates each event?
(2) What limits performance in each?
(3) How should each train?

step_by_step.calculation:
Athlete A (10 sec): CP system dominates (50-70%), glycolysis provides 30-40%.
Athlete B (2 min): Glycolysis dominates (50-60%), CP (10-20%), oxidative (20-30%).
Athlete C (4 hours): Oxidative dominates (>95%).
Athlete A: CP depletion (after 10 sec), neural factors. Athlete B: Lactate accumulation (15-20 mM), pH drop (muscle acid). Athlete C: Glycogen depletion (after 90 min), dehydration, electrolyte loss.

Athlete A: Sprint training (short bursts, full recovery), plyometrics, strength training. Increase CP stores, improve neural recruitment.
Athlete B: Interval training (200-400 m repeats, active recovery). Increase glycolytic enzymes, buffering capacity, lactate threshold.
Athlete C: Long slow distance, tempo runs, interval training. Increase mitochondria, capillaries, fat oxidation, glycogen storage.

worked_examples.final_answer:
(A) Sprinter (10 sec): CP system dominant. Limit: CP depletion. Train: short sprints, strength.
(B) 800m runner (2 min): Glycolysis dominant. Limit: lactate accumulation, pH drop. Train: intervals, buffering capacity.
(C) Marathoner (4 hours): Oxidative dominant. Limit: glycogen depletion. Train: long runs, increase mitochondria, fat oxidation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Different sports use different energy systems. Training must match the dominant energy system to improve performance. Sprinters need CP system training; 800m runners need glycolysis training; marathoners need oxidative training.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Energy Systems During Exercise
sections.introduction: As you exercise, the energy systems turn on and shift dominance over time. Follow the flow from the start of a sprint to the finish of a marathon.

process_flow.description: Creatine phosphate provides most of the ATP for explosive starts.
process_flow.mechanism: Muscle contraction → ADP builds up → creatine kinase transfers phosphate from CP to ADP → ATP regenerated. CP stores drop quickly.
process_flow.result: CP depleted (70-80%) after 10 seconds; ATP maintained

image.alt: CP + ADP → ATP + creatine. CP stores high at start, low at 10 seconds.
image.caption: Step 1 (0-10 sec): CP system dominates. CP is converted to ATP quickly. After 10 seconds, CP stores are 70-80% depleted.

process_flow.description: As CP runs out, glycolysis becomes the main ATP source.
process_flow.mechanism: Glycogen → glucose → pyruvate → lactate. ATP produced. Lactate and H⁺ build up, pH drops.
process_flow.result: Lactate accumulates (10-25 mM); pH drops (7.0 → 6.4-6.6); fatigue develops

image.alt: Glycogen → lactate + ATP. Lactate and H⁺ accumulate, causing fatigue.
image.caption: Step 2 (10 sec-2 min): Glycolysis dominates. Glycogen is broken down to lactate, producing ATP. Lactate and H⁺ build up, causing fatigue.

process_flow.description: For sustained exercise, oxidative phosphorylation becomes the main ATP source.
process_flow.mechanism: Pyruvate (from sugar) or fatty acids enter mitochondria → oxygen used → large amount of ATP produced. Sugar used for higher intensity; fat used for lower intensity.
process_flow.result: Sustained ATP production; CO₂ and H₂O produced; glycogen depletion after 90 minutes

image.alt: Sugar/fat + oxygen → CO₂ + H₂O + large amount of ATP.
image.caption: Step 3 (>2 min): Oxidative system dominates. Sugar or fat + oxygen → ATP. This system can last for hours, limited only by fuel stores.

sections.definition: These are more advanced concepts about how muscles regulate energy and what happens when energy systems fail.

process_types.energy_profile: AMPK (AMP-activated protein kinase) is a protein in your cells that acts like a fuel gauge. When energy is low (high AMP, low ATP), AMPK turns on and tells your cells to make more energy.
process_types.direction: Exercise → ATP used → AMP rises → AMPK activated → AMPK turns on glucose uptake, fat burning, and mitochondrial biogenesis (via PGC-1α).
process_types.function: AMPK helps your cells adapt to energy demands. It's why exercise makes your muscles better at making energy over time.
goals: Understand how cells sense energy levels
Learn why exercise changes muscles at the molecular level
Appreciate the link between energy and adaptation

primary_example.explanation:
When you exercise, your muscles use ATP, and AMP levels rise. AMPK senses this and kicks into action:
(1) During exercise: AMPK activates glucose uptake (so your muscles get more sugar from blood) and fat burning.
(2) After exercise: AMPK turns on a protein called PGC-1α, which is the "master switch" for building more mitochondria and more capillaries.
(3) Over weeks: Your muscles build more power plants (mitochondria), more blood vessels, and become better at using fat.

This is how exercise changes your muscles at the cellular level. It's not just about getting bigger—it's about changing the machinery inside your cells to become more efficient.

Fun fact: Metformin, a common diabetes drug, works by activating AMPK. That's why it helps lower blood sugar.

image.alt: Diagram: Exercise → AMP rises → AMPK activates → turns on glucose uptake, fat burning, PGC-1α → more mitochondria.
image.caption: AMPK is the energy sensor. When muscles use ATP, AMP rises and AMPK activates. It turns on glucose uptake, fat burning, and mitochondrial biogenesis—this is how exercise changes your muscles.

image.alt: AMPK activates PGC-1α → more mitochondria, more capillaries, better fat burning.
image.caption: AMPK activates PGC-1α, the master switch for building more mitochondria and capillaries. This is the molecular basis of endurance training.

process_types.energy_profile: Lactate produced during intense exercise isn't waste. Your liver can turn it back into glucose through a process called the Cori cycle.
process_types.direction: Muscle (glycolysis) → lactate → blood → liver → gluconeogenesis → glucose → blood → muscle
process_types.function: The Cori cycle recycles lactate, preventing loss of carbon and providing glucose to muscles during recovery.
goals: Understand that lactate is not just waste
Learn how the body recycles energy
Appreciate the connection between muscle and liver

primary_example.process: The Cori Cycle: Lactate to Glucose
primary_example.explanation:
After a hard run, your blood lactate levels are high. But that lactate doesn't just sit there—it's recycled.
(1) Lactate from muscles travels to the liver.
(2) In the liver, lactate is converted to pyruvate, then to glucose (a process called gluconeogenesis).
(3) The new glucose goes back into the blood, where muscles can use it to replenish glycogen.

Cost: This process uses energy (6 ATP per glucose), but it's worth it because it recycles the carbon from lactate. Without it, you'd lose valuable fuel.

What about the burn? The burning sensation during intense exercise is caused by H⁺ (acid), not lactate itself. Lactate is actually a fuel for your heart and slow-twitch fibers. So lactate is not the enemy—it's a shuttle moving energy from fast-twitch fibers to other tissues.

Recovery: Active recovery (light walking, cycling) speeds lactate clearance by keeping blood flowing and delivering lactate to the liver and heart for use.

image.alt: Diagram: Muscle lactate → blood → liver → glucose → blood → muscle glycogen.
image.caption: The Cori cycle: Lactate from muscles is converted back to glucose in the liver. This recycles carbon and provides fuel for glycogen replenishment.

image.alt: Lactate from Type IIb fibers goes to Type I fibers and heart as fuel.
image.caption: Lactate is not waste—it's a fuel. Fast-twitch fibers produce lactate; slow-twitch fibers and the heart use it as energy. This is the lactate shuttle.

sections.relationship: Muscle energetics is about how your muscles produce ATP to contract. You have three energy systems: creatine phosphate (immediate, 5-10 sec), glycolysis (short-term, 30-90 sec, produces lactate), and oxidative phosphorylation (long-term, sustained, uses oxygen). These systems work together, with dominance shifting based on exercise intensity and duration. Type I fibers are built for endurance (oxidative), Type IIb for power (glycolytic). Your muscles sense energy levels through AMPK, which turns on adaptations like building more mitochondria. Lactate from glycolysis is recycled by the liver through the Cori cycle. Metabolic disorders like McArdle disease (can't break down glycogen) and mitochondrial myopathies (defective power plants) show what happens when these systems fail. Understanding muscle energetics helps athletes train smarter, helps people understand fatigue, and reveals how exercise changes your body at the deepest level.

summary:
Muscles need ATP to contract. They have three energy systems to make ATP:

Creatine phosphate system (immediate, 5-10 sec):
• Reaction: CP + ADP → creatine + ATP.
• Very fast, lasts 5-10 seconds.
• No waste products.
• Used for: 100m sprint, jumping, heavy lifting.
• Recovery: 20-30 sec for half, 3-5 min for full.
• Creatine supplements can increase CP stores 10-20%.

Glycolysis (short-term, 30-90 sec):
• Breaks down glycogen to lactate.
• Produces 2-3 ATP per sugar molecule.
• Produces lactate and H⁺ (acid), causing pH drop and fatigue.
• Used for: 400m run, 100m swim, repeated sprints.
• Lactate threshold: trained athletes have higher threshold (70-85% vs 50-60% untrained).

Oxidative phosphorylation (long-term, sustained):
• Uses oxygen to break down sugar or fat in mitochondria.
• Produces 34-36 ATP per sugar, over 100 per fat.
• Produces only CO₂ and water.
• Used for: distance running, cycling, hiking.
• Endurance training increases mitochondria (50-100%), capillaries, and fat-burning ability.

Fiber type specialization:
• Type I (slow-twitch): many mitochondria, oxidative, good for endurance.
• Type IIb (fast-twitch): lots of glycogen, glycolytic, good for power.
• Type IIa: mixed properties.

Key principles:
• Power vs. capacity trade-off: fastest system has smallest capacity.
• Fiber type matches function: postural muscles have more Type I; eye muscles have more Type II.
• Oxygen debt (EPOC): extra oxygen after exercise to replenish CP, clear lactate, and restore oxygen.

Regulation:
• AMPK senses low energy and turns on glucose uptake, fat burning, and mitochondrial biogenesis.
• PGC-1α is the master switch for endurance adaptations.

Clinical disorders:
• McArdle disease: can't break down muscle glycogen → exercise intolerance, cramps, second wind phenomenon.
• Mitochondrial myopathies: defective mitochondria → exercise intolerance, fatigue, high lactate, ragged red fibers on biopsy.

Lactate is not waste:
• Cori cycle: liver converts lactate to glucose.
• Lactate shuttle: fast-twitch fibers produce lactate; slow-twitch fibers and heart use it as fuel.

Understanding muscle energetics helps athletes train appropriately (sprinters train CP system; 800m runners train glycolysis; marathoners train oxidative), helps people understand fatigue, and explains why different people excel at different sports.
